title: Chapter 9 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of the Context API!

questions.question: What problem does the Context API solve?
options: State management for local component state
questions.explanation: Context provides a way to share data without passing props through intermediate components.

questions.question: Context is a replacement for all state management; you no longer need useState.
questions.explanation: No, Context is for passing data, not for managing state. You still need useState or useReducer to create updatable data.

questions.question: Which function creates a context object?
questions.explanation: `React.createContext(defaultValue)` creates a context object.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Provider component?
options: To provide a default value to the context
To wrap components and make the context value available to them
questions.explanation: The Provider supplies the context value to all descendants.

questions.question: You can only pass one value per context.
questions.explanation: You can pass any JavaScript value, including objects with multiple fields.

questions.question: Which hook is used to consume context in a functional component?
questions.explanation: `useContext(ContextObject)` returns the current context value.

questions.question: If a component is not wrapped in a Provider, `useContext` returns the _____ value.
questions.explanation: It returns the default value passed to `createContext`.

questions.question: How can you make context updatable?
options: By passing a setter function as part of the context value
By using useReducer inside the context
By combining context with useState in the Provider
questions.explanation: All are valid ways to provide update mechanisms through context.

questions.question: When the context value changes, only the components that directly use that value re-render.
questions.explanation: All components that consume the context (use useContext) re-render, regardless of which part of the value they use.

questions.question: What is a good practice to avoid unnecessary re-renders with context?
options: Use one giant context for everything
Split unrelated data into separate contexts
questions.explanation: Splitting contexts prevents changes in one domain from affecting components that don't care about it.

questions.question: The _____ component is used to provide a value to the context.
questions.explanation: `Context.Provider` supplies the value.

questions.question: What will `useContext(MyContext)` return if there is no Provider above?
questions.explanation: It returns the default value provided to `createContext`.

questions.question: You can only use context with functional components, not class components.
questions.explanation: Class components can use `Context.Consumer` or `static contextType`.

questions.question:
What does the following code do?

```jsx
const MyContext = React.createContext();
```
options: Creates a context with default value undefined
Creates a context with default value null
Creates a context with no default value
questions.explanation: The default value is undefined if not provided.

questions.question: Context is best suited for data that is used by many components at different nesting levels, like user authentication or theme.
questions.explanation: Yes, global data like theme, user, locale are classic use cases.

questions.question: How can you memoize the context value to prevent unnecessary re-renders of the Provider's children?
questions.explanation: `useMemo` can be used to memoize the value object so it only changes when dependencies change.

questions.question: When a context value changes, React will re-render all _____ of that context.
questions.explanation: All components that use `useContext` on that context are consumers.

questions.question: Which pattern is used to provide both state and updater functions through context?
options: Passing an object with state and dispatch
Passing separate contexts for state and updater
questions.explanation: All these patterns are used; the object pattern is common.

questions.question: You can nest Providers to override values for a subtree.
questions.explanation: Yes, a deeper Provider with a new value will override the one above for its subtree.

questions.question: What is the main drawback of using a single context for many different values?
options: It causes unnecessary re-renders when any value changes
questions.explanation: All consumers re-render when any part of the context value changes, even if they don't use that part.
